title: Oklahoma State University — Watershed Hydrology & Rangeland Ecology (2 MS positions)
org: Stillwater, OK · Environmental Science Graduate Program
deadline: May 15, 2026 deadline passed — next cycle ~May 2027
body: Two fully funded MS assistantships — Neosho River Watershed, Oklahoma. Tar Creek Superfund Site contamination context. Position 1: Ecology/Rangeland — wildlife habitat, species of conservation need. Position 2: Hydrology/GIS — site suitability modeling, flood mitigation. Full stipend + tuition waiver + health insurance. Advisors: Dr. Scott Stoodley & Dr. Jaime Schussler. Email: esgp@okstate.edu
desc: Two fully funded MS assistantships for an interdisciplinary watershed conservation project in the Neosho River Watershed of northeastern Oklahoma — which includes the Tar Creek Superfund Site, one of the most historically significant mining-contaminated landscapes in the US. Position 1 (Ecology/Rangeland): wildlife habitat assessment, species of conservation need, restoration planning. Position 2 (Hydrology/GIS): watershed hydrology, site suitability modeling, flood mitigation, conservation planning. The contaminated watershed + GIS + field ecology combination is a direct methodological parallel to Erin’s Pigeon River lead contamination capstone. Advisors: Dr. Scott Stoodley & Dr. Jaime Schussler.
metaLine: Start: May 27–June 5, 2026  ·  Funding: Full stipend + tuition waiver + health insurance

detail.v: Confirmed open position (Aug 2026)

The Landscape Modeling Lab seeks a graduate student for a funded project on remote sensing models of wetland conversion and loss in the Prairie Pothole Region (ND, SD, MN, IA, MO). Fieldwork includes vegetation surveys, water sampling, and near-surface remote sensing. The student will lead a field team and coordinate with landowners for site access. Degree: MS or PhD in Ecosystem Science & Sustainability or Graduate Degree Program in Ecology.

title: University of Georgia — Odum School / SREL
org: Athens, GA / Aiken, SC
body: UGA's Odum School of Ecology and Savannah River Ecology Lab both offer competitive funded MS positions. Research in aquatic ecology, contaminant biogeochemistry, and wetland systems aligns strongly with Erin's coursework. Strong institutional reputation. Contact faculty directly.
extbody: About SREL The Savannah River Ecology Laboratory has operated for over 70 years as a field station near Aiken, SC. Research spans aquatic ecology, herpetology, radioecology, and ecosystem science. Graduate students enroll in UGA's Odum School of Ecology or related departments while conducting research at the SREL facility. Research areas matching Erin's background Contaminant biogeochemistry and trace metal movement through wetland systems; aquatic-terrestrial linkages in southeastern ecosystems
desc: SREL is a long-established field station in Aiken, SC with strong aquatic and wetland ecology research. MS opportunities in terrestrial-aquatic linkages and contaminant ecology. Competitive stipend and tuition waiver; contact faculty directly to confirm current openings.

title: University of Southern Mississippi — Gulf Coast Research Lab
org: Hattiesburg/Ocean Springs, MS · Marine Science
body: Gulf Coast Research Lab has MS assistantships in Marine Science including oyster recruitment and coastal restoration research in Mississippi Sound. Good match for field sampling and aquatic ecology background. Verify current openings directly with GCRL faculty.
more: About GCRL The Gulf Coast Research Laboratory in Ocean Springs, MS is one of the premier marine science research stations on the Gulf Coast. Graduate students in the MS in Marine Science program are based at GCRL with access to coastal and estuarine research sites throughout Mississippi Sound and the northern Gulf. Research match Coastal restoration ecology, oyster reef dynamics, and estuarine water quality are active research areas. Erin's soil/water sampling skills, aquatic ecology background, and toxicology coursework are directly applicable to field work in these systems. Browse faculty at usm.edu/gcrl/faculty-staff and contact faculty whose work aligns with water quality or coastal ecology interests.

Vegetation and wildlife inventory methods. Co-advised by Drs. Jodi Forrester & Chris Moorman (NC State) and Dr. Tara Keyser (USDA FS). This ...
extbody: Funded MS position (preferred start May 2026) studying Hurricane Helene forest damage and recovery in western NC Pisgah National Forest oak-hickory forests. Vegetation and wildlife inventory methods. Co-advised by Drs. Jodi Forrester & Chris Moorman (NC State) and Dr. Tara Keyser (USDA FS). This is the single best match for Erin's background.

links.label: 🔗 NC State FER — Graduate Application Process

more: Why this is Erin's #1 match This project studies the same Hurricane Helene event that Erin researched for her senior capstone — just from a forest ecology rather than water quality angle. The field site (Pisgah National Forest near Asheville) is adjacent to where Erin conducted her Pigeon River Basin research. Her WCU background, Appalachian field experience, wildlife coursework, and NRM 479 mammalogy skills all directly support this position. She can honestly say she has studied the same event from a complementary angle — that's a compelling narrative. StipendFunded (rate TBD; NC State MS typically $18k–$23k) TuitionFull waiver StartMay 2026 preferred GRENot required by NC State 📅 Fall application deadline (aid)January 31 for financial aid; June 25 admission only 📅 Spring deadlineSeptember 30 (aid); November 25 (admission only) ⚠ Contact professor firstEmail Dr. Forrester to confirm position availability before applying Email Dr. Jodi Forrester: jodi_forrester@ncsu.eduSingle PDF: letter of interest + CV + unofficial transcripts + 3 references. Subject: "Hurricane Helene MS position inquiry." Lead with the Pigeon River research connection in your opening paragraph.
desc: Actively recruiting a funded MS student (preferred start May 2026) to study how forest structure, composition, and function are affected by Hurricane Helene in western NC oak-hickory forests. Uses vegetation and wildlife inventories to evaluate and aid forest recovery. Co-advised by Drs. Jodi Forrester and Chris Moorman (NC State) and Dr. Tara Keyser (USDA Forest Service). This is a near-perfect match for Erin's background and her Pigeon River research.

extbody: The Elmore Lab at Clemson's Forestry & Environmental Conservation dept. was recruiting MS and PhD students for a Hurricane Helene bird ecology project. MS candidate to coordinate nest monitoring studying how priority bird species (Kentucky Warbler, Golden-winged Warbler, Cerulean Warbler, Swainson's Warbler, Ruffed Grouse) respond to hurricane damage. MS stipend $30k/yr; PhD $35k/yr. Field work in Pisgah National Forest. Verify if still open.

links.label: 🔗 PhD & MS posting — Bird response to disturbance (Elmore Lab)

more: Position details (verify current status) The Elmore Lab was actively recruiting for both a PhD and MS student. The MS position involves coordinating a nest monitoring project studying nesting behavior and success of priority bird species in hurricane-disturbed vs. undisturbed forest areas. Students will have the opportunity to shape their thesis questions within the monitoring framework. Compensation package (if still available) MS stipend$30,000/yr (12-month) TuitionFull waiver ExtrasUniversity computer, conference/travel funding, publication costs Field locationPisgah National Forest, near Asheville, NC 📅 ApplicationRolling — apply immediately; review began immediately, continues until filled Start dateJanuary or May 2026 (verify if still open) Fit assessment Good match for Erin's NRM 479 (Mammalogy) field skills and NRM 330 wildlife management background. Bird focus is adjacent — she would need to convey transferable field skills. The Hurricane Helene connection and Appalachian fieldwork at WCU are strong talking points. Contact: jaelmor@clemson.edu with subject "Bird response to disturbance graduate position." Send: letter of interest, CV, unofficial transcripts, and 3 references as a single PDF. Note: position review began immediately — confirm availability first.

body: Three-year funded MS research assistantship starting January 2026 in the Forest Hydrology and Soils Lab (Dr. Courtney Siegert). NSF-FIRE project investigating how back-to-back extreme weather events drive forest hydrology and flammability in the southeastern US. Fully funded: full tuition waiver ...
extbody: Three-year funded MS research assistantship starting January 2026 in the Forest Hydrology and Soils Lab (Dr. Courtney Siegert). NSF-FIRE project investigating how back-to-back extreme weather events drive forest hydrology and flammability in the southeastern US. Fully funded: full tuition waiver + $22k stipend + health insurance + conference travel support.

links.label: 🔗 Dr. Courtney Siegert — Faculty page (MSU Forestry)
🔗 Siegert Hydrolab — Forest Hydrology & Soils (lab site)

more: Project details This is an NSF-FIRE funded project investigating how "whiplashes" (back-to-back extreme wet/dry events) in moisture patterns drive forest hydrology and flammability across southeastern landscapes. The student will work with an interdisciplinary team at MSU, NC State, and the USDA Forest Service. Work includes quantifying regional fuel loadings and conducting field-scale fuel moisture dynamic experiments. Funding Stipend$22,000/yr Duration3 years fully funded TuitionFull waiver Health insuranceIncluded Conference travelSupplemental funding available Start dateJanuary 2026 (may be filled — verify; ask about future openings) 📅 Fall 2026 deadlineDecember 1 domestic / October 1 international (for Dr. Siegert lab) ⚠ Contact firstEmail Dr. Siegert before applying to confirm funding availability Fit assessment Strong match for Erin's hydrology and water quality background (ENVH 310, GEOL 423), forest ecosystems exposure (NRM 330, NRM 351), and field experience. Applying to environmental science, hydrology, or forestry backgrounds explicitly welcome. If this specific position is filled, contact Dr. Siegert about future openings or related projects. Dr. Courtney Siegert — courtney.siegert@msstate.eduSubject: "Application for Fire MS Assistantship." Send CV + brief statement of research experience. Ask about January 2026 status and any upcoming openings.

title: Dauphin Island Sea Lab — BOATS Postbac Fellowship
org: Dauphin Island, AL · Mobile Bay estuary / northern Gulf of Mexico
deadline: Application window open March 30 – August 21, 2026; program Jan 4 – Dec 17, 2027
body: NSF-funded postbac fellowship at Dauphin Island Sea Lab. $46K stipend + housing + research funds. One year of mentored research in coastal/marine science. Eligible within 2.5 years of BS degree. Priority for underrepresented groups and lower-resourced institutions. Apply at disl.edu/university-programs/boats/
desc: NSF-funded postbaccalaureate research fellowship at Alabama’s premier marine science research center, located on a Gulf barrier island adjacent to Mobile Bay. One full year of immersive, mentored research in coastal and marine science. Faculty mentors include researchers in fisheries ecology (Dr. Sean Powers), marine ecology/restoration, microbial ecology, and coastal biogeochemistry. Eligibility: BS degree within past 2.5 years (Erin eligible through late 2027), minimum 3.0 GPA. Explicitly prioritizes underrepresented groups and graduates of lower-resourced institutions. Strong fit for Erin’s aquatic/water quality background and goal of building credentials for graduate school. Apply at disl.edu/university-programs/boats/.

detail.v: About the program

The USGS Recent Graduate Program is a formal federal hiring pathway. Participants complete a structured 1–2 year developmental experience, receive mentoring, and can be non-competitively converted to permanent federal positions at the end. This is a meaningful career accelerator for someone targeting a federal water resources or environmental science career.
How to find the right position

Search USAJobs for "USGS Recent Graduate" and filter by series: 1315 (Hydrology), 1350 (Geology), 0401 (Biology), 0408 (Ecology). Also search 1301 (Physical Science). Prioritize Water Science Center positions — SAWSC (NC/SC/GA), Southeast (FL/AL/MS), and Appalachians WSC are the strongest regional fits.

detail.label: ⚠ Federal resume note
detail.value: Federal resumes are longer than private sector — include GPA, all relevant coursework, and specific technical skills. USAJobs has a resume builder tool.

desc: The USGS Recent Graduate Program provides 1–2 year paid developmental positions for graduates within the last 2 years, convertible to permanent federal employment. Erin graduated May 2025 — she remains eligible through May 2027. SAWSC covers exactly the NC/SC/GA region where she conducted her Pigeon River research. Hydrologist (GS-1315) and Physical Scientist (GS-1301) series are the strongest fits; also search Biological Science (GS-0401) and Ecology (GS-0408).

detail.v: How to maximize success

Search USAJobs for "USGS Recent Graduate" and filter by series: 1315 (Hydrology), 1350 (Geology), 0401 (Biology), 0408 (Ecology), 1301 (Physical Science). Prioritize SAWSC (NC/SC/GA), Southeast WSC (FL/AL/MS), and Appalachians WSC. Email gs-sawscsc@usgs.gov directly — SAWSC explicitly welcomes direct contact for upcoming vacancies.

detail.label: Federal resume tip
detail.value: Include GPA (3.731), all relevant coursework, specific duties from Pigeon River project, hours/week. Federal resumes are longer — use USAJobs resume builder.

detail.label: Key credential
detail.value: GEOL 423 (Contaminated Rivers) is cross-listed at the 500 (graduate) level — note this explicitly in the resume and cover letter

OWOW (Office of Wetlands, Oceans and Watersheds) — protects wetlands, monitors water quality, funds aquatic restoration, and develops watershed management plans. Entry-level Natural Resource Specialist and Biologist positions process permit applications, conduct wetland delineation reviews, and coordinate with state agencies. This is the most direct institutional match for Erin's Army Corps delineation training.

detail.v: Why the Wilmington District is the best fit

The USACE Wilmington District has jurisdiction over NC and SC — the exact geography where Erin studied wetlands and conducted her field research. Regulatory staff review Section 404 Clean Water Act wetland permit applications from developers, evaluate wetland delineation reports, and make jurisdictional determinations. Erin's GEOL 455 coursework used the Army Corps Delineation Manual directly — she would come in with working knowledge of the methodology these staff use every day.
How to stand out

Reference GEOL 455 prominently: "coursework using the Army Corps of Engineers Wetland Delineation Manual and Regional Supplements for the Eastern Mountains and Piedmont." Mention participation in Panthertown Valley field trips and the Carolina wetland community types covered in the course.

desc: Environmental consulting firms hire Staff Environmental Scientists (sometimes called Environmental Scientist I or Junior Ecologist) with BS degrees and wetland/GIS/field skills. Duties include conducting wetland delineations, writing delineation reports, preparing permit applications, conducting wildlife surveys, and supporting NEPA documentation. These firms hire year-round and operate in both the Southeast and Colorado. Starting salary typically $42k–$52k depending on location.

detail.v: Which firms to target
ECS Federal — large multi-disciplinary firm active in Carolinas and mid-Atlantic; strong wetland delineation practice.
Terracon — nationwide consulting; offices in Charlotte, Raleigh, and Colorado; environmental science and geotechnical focus.
Stantec — global firm; active coastal engineering internship (Summer 2026) in Austin; also NC and CO offices.
Kimley-Horn — major Raleigh-headquartered firm with strong environmental practice in SE; known for hiring WCU, NCSU, and App State graduates.
Brown and Caldwell / Arcadis — water-focused firms; environmental scientist positions in Raleigh and Colorado regularly.
What distinguishes Erin's application

Army Corps delineation training (GEOL 455) is a direct credential for wetland consulting roles. Most entry-level candidates have biology or environmental science degrees but haven't actually used the Wetland Delineation Manual. Erin has. That's worth leading with in cover letters to consulting firms.

Email gs-sawscsc@usgs.gov with a 2-paragraph cover note and resume — SAWSC explicitly maintains a contact list for upcoming vacancies. title: Biological Science Technician IP — Cherokee & Pisgah National Forests
org: Southeast Conservation Corps / US Forest Service · Eastern TN / Western NC
body: Southeast Conservation Corps regularly places AmeriCorps members with US Forest Service fisheries and wildlife teams in Cherokee National Forest (TN) and Pisgah National Forest (NC) — the exact landscape of Erin's Hurricane Helene research. Duties include aquatic resource monitoring, stream resto...
desc: Southeast Conservation Corps regularly places AmeriCorps members with US Forest Service fisheries and wildlife teams in Cherokee National Forest (TN) and Pisgah National Forest (NC) — the exact landscape of Erin's Hurricane Helene research. Duties include aquatic resource monitoring, stream restoration, electrofishing, macroinvertebrate surveys, and freshwater snorkeling. This is a direct follow-on to her GEOL 423 (Contaminated Rivers) and ENVH 310 (Water Quality) coursework in the same geography.

detail.v: Why this is strategically strong

A SECC IP in Pisgah NF or Cherokee NF builds directly on Erin's WCU field experience and Pigeon River research. USFS contacts made during service are exactly the kind of professional network that leads to graduate school advisor relationships — Dr. Tara Keyser (USDA Forest Service Southern Research Station, co-advisor on the NC State Hurricane Helene MS position) works in this region. An SECC placement here could facilitate a direct introduction.

desc: American Youthworks places AmeriCorps members at the Wichita Mountains Wildlife Refuge and Fort Sill through the ARTES (At Risk, Threatened, and Endangered Species) Program — monitoring tricolored bat, monarch butterfly, black-capped vireo, and northern bobwhite quail. The 2026 cycle (March 2 – Nov 6) had a January 25 deadline; the 2026–27 version will follow a similar timeline. 1,200-hour term provides full Segal education award (~$7,395). $600/week living allowance + $225/week housing stipend.

detail.v: Why the full 1,200-hour term matters

A 1,200-hour AmeriCorps term earns the full Segal Education Award (~$7,395 in 2025–26) and qualifies for Public Lands Corps Hiring Authority. The housing stipend reduces cost-of-living burden significantly. Erin's NRM 479 (Mammalogy) — including bat identification and small mammal trapping — directly matches the tricolored bat and small mammal monitoring duties here.

org: US Environmental Protection Agency · Nationwide + EPA facility summer internship
deadline: Typically fall annually
body: The EPA GRO Fellowship covers tuition and provides a stipend for students in environmental careers, including a paid summer internship at an EPA facility. Designed for undergraduate seniors and graduate students pursuing degrees in environmentally-related fields. If Erin enrolls in a graduate pro...
badge: Watch — falls open annually in fall
desc: The EPA GRO Fellowship covers tuition and provides a stipend for students in environmental careers, including a paid summer internship at an EPA facility. Designed for undergraduate seniors and graduate students pursuing degrees in environmentally-related fields. If Erin enrolls in a graduate program for Fall 2027, she could apply for GRO in fall 2026 for the 2027–28 cycle. Strong profile match: her toxicology, epidemiology, and water quality background directly aligns with EPA's environmental health research focus.

detail.v: How to position Erin's application

Lead with ENVH 375 (Environmental Toxicology, A+), HSCC 472 (Epidemiology, A+), GEOL 423 (Contaminated Rivers), and the Pigeon River lead contamination capstone. These four credentials are a direct match for EPA's environmental health research focus. The environmental justice coursework (ENVH 493, Appalachian context) is an additional differentiator.

detail.label: Timing
detail.value: Apply fall 2026 if enrolled in or admitted to a graduate program for Fall 2027

desc: SERC's Global Change Ecology and Biogeochemistry Labs recruit research technicians for coastal ecosystem and wetland response research (~$46k/yr, 6-month to 1-year appointments). Current and recent openings have focused on root and rhizome processing from long-term wetland experiments — field and lab work aligned with Erin's soil and water quality background. Strong technical credentialing for graduate school. Watch SERC careers and ecophys-jobs.org for upcoming fall 2026 postings.

links.label: 🔗 SERC Research Technician — Global Change Ecology & Biogeochemistry

detail.v: Why it matters for grad school

A year as a research technician at a major research institution like SERC with a peer-reviewed publication or conference presentation is one of the strongest possible graduate school application boosters. SERC has active relationships with major graduate programs (Maryland, Virginia, JHU). Technicians often get co-authorship on papers and strong recommendation letters from PI researchers.

detail.label: Field skills match
detail.value: Soil sampling, root/rhizome processing, wetland experiment maintenance, water sampling — all in Erin's GEOL 455 and ENVH 310 toolkit

title: USGS — Recent Graduates Program (environmental science / GIS)
org: U.S. Geological Survey · nationwide (USAJOBS)
deadline: Rolling — posted year-round on USAJOBS; no fixed date
body: Federal Pathways Recent Graduates Program: 1-year paid developmental positions across USGS science centers (hydrology, water quality, GIS, ecology). A 2025 BS grad is eligible through 2027. Postings appear year-round on USAJOBS; can convert to permanent. Contact: pathways@usgs.gov
desc: USGS Recent Graduates Program (federal Pathways) places recent graduates (within 2 years of a qualifying degree — a 2025 BS grad qualifies through 2027) into one-year paid developmental positions at USGS science centers nationwide, with a mentor, an Individual Development Plan, and at least 40 hours of formal training. Roles span water resources, hydrology, water quality, GIS/remote sensing, and ecology — a strong match for Erin’s water-quality and GIS background. Successful completion can convert noncompetitively to a permanent federal position. Opportunities are posted year-round on USAJOBS (USGS portal); there is no single fixed deadline — set a USAJOBS saved search and apply as roles post.
metaLine: 1-year paid appointment · mentor + training · converts to permanent · posted year-round

title: University of Vermont — Funded PhD, Lake Champlain Fisheries (Henderson Lab / VT Coop Unit)
org: University of Vermont — USGS Vermont Cooperative Fish & Wildlife Research Unit · Henderson Lab · Dr. Mark Henderson · Burlington, VT
deadline: Contact-first — email Dr. Mark Henderson (mark.henderson@uvm.edu) to ask about current openings and the application timeline.
body: Two funded PhD assistantships in the Henderson Lab at the USGS Vermont Cooperative Fish & Wildlife Research Unit (Rubenstein School of Environment and Natural Resources). Project 1 uses acoustic telemetry and genomic analyses to test whether multiple Lake Sturgeon populations exist within Lake Champlain and to estimate their effective population size. Project 2 uses acoustic telemetry and genomics to resolve population structure, spawning behavior, and habitat use of Lake Whitefish in Lake Champlain, testing for ecotypes that disproportionately contribute to the spawning population. Positions include a competitive stipend, tuition, and health insurance.
desc: Lake Champlain fisheries work pairing acoustic telemetry and field monitoring with population genomics. The telemetry, spawning, habitat-use, and population-structure components align well with Erin's field-monitoring background (camera-trap and small-mammal survey experience) and her aquatic interests, while the genomics emphasis would be new ground for her. These are PhD positions — a direct step up from a BS — so they fit if she's ready to commit to a doctorate; the cooperative-unit funding model (stipend + tuition + health insurance) makes for a strong package. Contact-first: a short email to Dr. Henderson about her aquatic monitoring and Pigeon River capstone is the right opening.

title: California BLM Riparian & Wetland AIM Monitoring — Crew Leads & Field Technicians (CNHP)
org: Colorado Natural Heritage Program (Colorado State University) · in partnership with California BLM · statewide, CA
deadline: Seasonal summer crew (recurring). The 2026 field season is already underway — email a resume, cover letter, and three references (with phone and email) to Katrina Castro (Katrina.Castro@colostate.edu) to be considered for the next (2027) season, and note if you want Crew Lead consideration. Watch CNHP's jobs page and the TAMU Natural Resources Job Board in late winter.
body: Field crews collect wetland and riparian condition data using BLM's Riparian & Wetland Assessment, Inventory, and Monitoring (AIM) protocol on BLM lands across California. Crews are trained by CNHP and BLM National Operations Center wetland ecologists, then spend the season sampling — vegetation composition, soil-profile descriptions, and water-quality measurements — beginning in southeastern California and moving to northeastern California (Eagle Lake and Applegate Field Offices). Crew Leads require a B.S. and lead a small crew; Field Technicians assist with data collection and backcountry navigation. Per recent CNHP announcements: Crew Leads roughly $20–22/hr and Field Technicians roughly $17–19/hr, depending on experience, plus about $60/day per diem and gear reimbursement (around $395–455 per two-week hitch) when traveling beyond the base location.
desc: A near-exact match to Erin's field toolkit — wetland delineation (she is trained in the Army Corps manual), vegetation sampling, soil-profile description, and water-quality/sediment sampling are the core daily duties. The AIM protocol is a nationally standardized federal monitoring method that reads strongly on a federal or grad-school résumé, and the Crew Lead role is open to a B.S. graduate. West Coast placement also broadens her geographic range. The main caveats: it is a seasonal field position rather than a career track, and the 2026 season is already filled — so this is a plan-ahead card for the 2027 cycle (or an immediate contact if any late vacancies open).

title: University of Connecticut — Funded PhD, Biogeochemistry of Beaver-Created Ecosystems (Helton Lab)
org: University of Connecticut — Dept. of Natural Resources & the Environment · Helton & Lawrence Labs · Dr. Ashley Helton · Storrs, CT
deadline: Apply by September 1, 2026 — email Dr. Ashley Helton (ashley.helton@uconn.edu) with the subject line "PhD Application Beaver Project," a research statement, and a CV listing three references.
body: Fully funded PhD Graduate Assistantship (up to $40,000/yr) in the Helton and Lawrence lab groups, studying how beaver-created ecosystems in the northeastern United States affect freshwater carbon cycling. The project pairs field biogeochemistry with modeling to quantify how beaver ponds and wetlands store and release carbon. A bachelor's degree is required and about one year of post-baccalaureate research experience is expected; a master's is strongly preferred but not required. To apply, email a research statement and a CV (with three references) to Dr. Ashley Helton.
desc: Freshwater biogeochemistry and wetland carbon cycling sit squarely in Erin's interest areas, and the work builds naturally on her Pigeon River trace-metal sediment capstone and sediment-sampling skills — sediment and water biogeochemistry are the same analytical world. Beaver-pond wetlands also connect to her wetland-delineation training, and the stipend is excellent (up to $40k). It is a direct-to-PhD position where the ad prefers (but does not require) a master's and expects roughly a year of post-baccalaureate research experience — so eligibility is a reach-to-strong depending on how her AmeriCorps and technician research time is counted. A specific, well-argued outreach email to Dr. Helton is the right move; the later start (early–mid 2027) leaves time to line up references and funding.

title: Cal Poly Humboldt — Three Funded MS Assistantships, Fisheries Conservation Genetics & eDNA (Arcata, CA)
org: Cal Poly Humboldt — Dept. of Fisheries Biology · Natural Resources MS · Arcata, CA (northern California)
deadline: Contact-first — email the Fisheries Biology graduate program (fisheries@humboldt.edu) to ask which of the three genetics/eDNA assistantships are still open and the current application timeline; the campus Natural Resources MS admission cycle typically closes in late winter for fall start.
start: Fall term (confirm year with the department)
body: Three funded M.S. Graduate Research Assistantships in the Fisheries Biology department at Cal Poly Humboldt (Arcata, CA), applying genetic and environmental-DNA (eDNA) approaches to fish conservation and management. The three projects are: (1) quantifying variability of Ceratonova shasta (a salmonid parasite) DNA concentrations in Klamath River water samples; (2) monitoring coho salmon at local scales in northern California streams using eDNA; and (3) using genomics to study local adaptation among isolated populations of the endangered tidewater goby. Support is roughly $25,000/yr (hourly wages or stipend depending on position) plus partial tuition support. A BS is sufficient to apply to the Natural Resources MS.
desc: This is a solid West Coast fisheries/aquatic add that leans on Erin's water-sampling and field-monitoring background — the Klamath River project in particular is built on collecting and analyzing river water samples, the same field-and-lab world as her Pigeon River trace-metal water/sediment work. The eDNA and genomics methods are new ground for her (her strengths are wetland delineation, GIS, sediment/trace-metal sampling, and camera/small-mammal work), so this is a genuine skills stretch rather than a bullseye — hence a Good rather than Strong tier. Because the three positions fill individually and no dated deadline is posted, the right move is a specific outreach email naming the project she is most drawn to (the Klamath water-quality/parasite project fits her best) and asking about the current cycle. Arcata is redwood-coast northern California, filling an under-represented West Coast region on her list.

body: MS and PhD study in the Watershed Management & Ecohydrology emphasis within the School of Natural Resources & the Environment at the University of Arizona (Tucson). The program trains students to manage water resources by understanding how hydrologic processes are shaped by land management and land-surface conditions in upland watersheds, especially in the arid Southwest; coursework spans dryland ecohydrology, vegetation dynamics, wildland water quality, ecological climatology, and fire ecology. Funding is typically through research or teaching assistantships tied to a faculty advisor's grants — the program page does not list a blanket funded offer, so funding should be confirmed directly with a prospective advisor. A BS is sufficient to apply for the MS.
desc: Topically this is close to a bullseye for Erin's stated interests — watershed science, hydrology, water quality, and GIS/remote sensing — applied to arid-Southwest watersheds, a landscape and water-scarcity context different from her Appalachian/Southeast experience and therefore good breadth. Her multi-criteria GIS suitability work and water-quality sampling transfer directly to ecohydrology and wildland water-quality research. The one caveat is funding: unlike a posted assistantship, admission-plus-funding here runs through a specific faculty advisor, so this is a contact-first program rather than a single deadline to hit — the move is to find one or two SNRE watershed/ecohydrology faculty whose work matches her wetland/water-quality background and email them before applying. title: Habitat Restoration Fellow — Bitter Lake National Wildlife Refuge (ACE EPIC / USFWS)
org: American Conservation Experience — EPIC · in partnership with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service · Bitter Lake NWR, Roswell, NM
deadline: Rolling — applications reviewed as received; the posted start date is August 31, 2026, so apply well before then. Apply through the ACE applicant portal.
body: A 52-week ACE EPIC fellowship at Bitter Lake National Wildlife Refuge, a 24,563-acre refuge in the Pecos River watershed that is among the most biologically significant wetland complexes in the Southwest. Roughly 75% of the work is native seed collection, propagation, and monitoring of restoration outcomes, together with invasive species removal. The remaining time covers fish, waterbird, and threatened-and-endangered species surveys plus monitoring of wetland water levels and flows. The position carries Public Land Corps (PLC) hiring-authority eligibility, which grants non-competitive federal hiring status after the required hours are served. The posted living allowance is $600 per week.
desc: This is the strongest single Southwest match on the board right now because it stacks three of Erin's core areas into one term: wetland systems, watershed hydrology, and ecological restoration. Monitoring wetland water levels and flows in the Pecos River watershed is a direct analogue to her Pigeon River watershed capstone, and the T&E and waterbird survey work builds on her camera-trap and field-monitoring experience. The decisive feature is the PLC non-competitive hiring authority — it is the cleanest documented path from a BS into the permanent USFWS positions she is targeting, and it stacks with her AmeriCorps NCCC alumna status. Caveat: it is a term fellowship, not a career appointment, and Roswell is a remote posting.

body: A 52-week, 1700-hour AmeriCorps individual placement revisiting permanent upland and riparian vegetation monitoring plots across roughly six National Park units in southern Arizona. Core duties include plant identification, soil stability and biological soil crust data collection, greenline riparian surveys, GPS-tablet mapping of non-native plants, and data QA/QC. The NPS Inventory & Monitoring networks run nationally standardized protocols, which reads well on both a federal application and a grad-school CV, and a second AmeriCorps term adds another Segal award toward tuition. As an NCCC alumna she already knows the service model. Caveats: the deadline is close, and the position is monitoring-focused rather than a research or career track.

org: Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (ADEQ) · Phoenix, AZ

body: A full-time ADEQ ambient monitoring role that is roughly 50% field time: collecting environmental samples, calibrating and troubleshooting monitoring instruments, and performing data verification under EPA Quality Assurance Handbook procedures and agency Quality Assurance Project Plans. The posting is open until filled and offers remote flexibility of up to two days per week. It is monitoring and compliance rather than ecology, so it is a career-foundation option rather than a wildlife-track one — but it pays a real salary and builds regulatory experience her field-heavy résumé currently lacks.

body: The professional-track arm of the Washington Conservation Corps. Rather than serving on a field crew, Individual Placement members are embedded one-on-one with a host organization's biologists, foresters, hydrologists, and environmental educators, taking on project-level responsibility within that agency or nonprofit. Terms run 11 months, October through September. The posted living allowance is $1,476 twice monthly (approximately $2,952 per month pre-tax), rising to $1,609 twice monthly in King, Snohomish, and Clark counties, plus a Segal AmeriCorps Education Award of up to $7,395. A college degree is preferred for many placements.
desc: This is the version of a corps term that actually rewards Erin's degree. Where a standard crew term would use her as field labor, an Individual Placement embeds her alongside a hydrologist or biologist doing project work — so her GIS suitability modeling, wetland delineation, and monitoring experience become the reason she is selected rather than background detail. Washington is a strong region for salmon, riparian, and wetland restoration work, the pay is at the top of the AmeriCorps range, and a second Segal award offsets grad-school cost. As an NCCC alumna she clears the culture bar immediately. Caveat: the specific host placement is matched during the process, so the technical content depends on which host she lands.

title: EPA Research Participation Program (ORISE) — Postgraduate Appointments (recurring source)
org: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency · administered by the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education (ORISE) · EPA labs and regional offices nationwide
deadline: Rolling — applications are accepted year-round and new opportunities post continuously. Individual postings carry their own closing dates and may close early once a strong applicant pool forms. Set up an alert on the ORISE opportunity catalog and check it monthly.
start: Varies by appointment (one year, renewable up to five years total)
body: This is a recurring SOURCE card rather than a single posting — ORISE administers EPA's research participation program and refreshes its catalog continuously, so the durable action is to monitor the catalog and apply to matching postings as they appear. Appointments place recent graduates on project-specific EPA research in water quality, wetlands and streams, ecological monitoring, and environmental data and GIS work. Postgraduate applicants qualify with a BS received within the last 60 months; U.S. citizenship is required, along with two recommendations. Appointments run one year and are renewable up to five years total, and all applications go through the Zintellect portal. Stipends are monthly and commensurate with education and experience, with a health insurance supplement; dollar amounts are stated on each individual posting. Recent EPA postings in this catalog have included Great Lakes ecosystem restoration fellowships and regional wetlands and streams reference-data work.
desc: Worth treating as a standing monthly check rather than a one-time application. EPA's wetlands, streams, and water-quality condition assessment postings are a direct application of Erin's Army Corps delineation training and trace-metal sediment sampling, and the BS-within-60-months eligibility rule places her squarely in the target window — a window that closes in 2030, so this is best used early. ORISE appointments are among the cleanest on-ramps into federal science for someone without prior federal service. Note that her list already includes item-113, a specific ORISE wetland plant ecology fellowship; this card is the broader catalog behind it. Caveats: stipends vary by posting and are not published centrally, and appointments are training positions rather than federal employment, so they do not confer competitive status by themselves.

deadline: Rolling — the posting notes it may close at any time, so apply promptly.
body: Four term positions running September 8, 2026 through July 13, 2027 on BLM-managed California desert lands. Members identify restoration project sites, monitor the outcomes of prior restoration efforts, maintain the GIS restoration database, coordinate volunteers and crews, and deliver outreach to off-highway-vehicle recreationists. The posting requires a valid driver's license and comfort working independently in remote settings. Posted benefits are an $875 per week living allowance, up to $225 per week in housing reimbursement, up to $150 in gear assistance, and up to $350 in training assistance.
desc: This posting explicitly pairs field restoration monitoring with GIS database maintenance — the two skills Erin has the most documented experience in — which is a less common combination than it sounds. Monitoring the outcomes of prior restoration work is genuinely useful experience for restoration-track graduate applications, since it teaches what actually succeeds rather than only how to install a project. The compensation is the highest weekly rate among the ACE placements found, and the housing reimbursement makes the effective pay stronger still. Caveats: the OHV recreation-outreach component is a public-facing role outside her experience, and the Mojave desert posting is remote and hot.

title: University of Hawaiʻi at Hilo — M.S. Tropical Conservation Biology & Environmental Science (TCBES) assistantships
org: University of Hawaiʻi at Hilo · Tropical Conservation Biology and Environmental Science program · Hilo, HI
deadline: Contact-first — graduate assistantships are posted through NEOGOV and awarded in one-year terms with the option to reapply. Email the TCBES program and prospective faculty advisors about assistantship availability before applying.
start: Fall (next available cohort — confirm with the program)
body: Standing graduate assistantships for prospective and current TCBES master's students, providing a monthly salary plus full tuition remission (excluding student fees), awarded in one-year terms with the option to reapply. Positions are posted through the NEOGOV system. The TCBES M.S. spans conservation biology, watershed and stream ecology, ecological restoration, and geospatial analysis, in one of the most distinctive island ecosystems available for conservation research. A BS is sufficient to apply for the M.S.
desc: A BS-eligible master's program with tuition-remission assistantships whose research areas — watershed and stream ecology, restoration, and geospatial analysis — line up closely with Erin's wetland delineation, water quality, and GIS skill set. Hawaiʻi island ecosystems offer a genuinely distinctive research context (endemism, invasive species pressure, and steep watershed gradients) that would differentiate her from mainland-trained applicants. The tuition remission is a real financial advantage. Honest caveats: assistantship dollar amounts are not published centrally so funding must be confirmed directly, and one important eligibility note — the parallel Hauʻoli Mau Loa Foundation assistantships require graduation from a Hawaiʻi high school and she is not eligible for those; the general TCBES awards carry no such restriction.
metaLine: M.S. · monthly salary + full tuition remission (amount unpublished) · Hilo HI · contact-first
location: Hilo, HI

stipend: Graduate assistantship, one-year terms, renewable by reapplication (amount unpublished)

Grant writing and project permitting are genuinely valuable professional skills she does not yet have, and running a district's programs would demonstrate leadership on a grad-school or federal application. Honest caveat: this is a manager role, so it would ask a 2025 graduate to lead programs and handle contracting immediately — a real stretch in responsibility even though she meets the stated qualifications.

org: Idaho Department of Fish and Game · Salmon Regional Office · Salmon, ID

body: A seasonal temporary technician position of roughly eight months at up to 40 hours per week, providing field support for bighorn sheep research and wildlife management in the Salmon region. Duties include field data collection, wildlife population surveys, Chronic Wasting Disease monitoring via biological sample collection, and work with marked animals. The work is remote and backcountry, involving substantial hiking and overnight stays. Minimum qualifications are knowledge of wildlife ecology plus biological data experience — a BS clears this with no master's or multi-year requirement. Posted pay is $18.61 per hour.
desc: A straightforward, currently-open field technician role that directly extends Erin's small-mammal trapping, camera-trap, and field-data background into large-mammal research. Bighorn sheep work in the Salmon region is genuinely rugged backcountry field experience, which is exactly what strengthens a wildlife-track grad application, and the CWD biological sampling adds a disease-surveillance skill that agencies increasingly want. The qualification bar sits comfortably below her degree, so she is a strong rather than marginal candidate. Caveats: it is seasonal and modestly paid, and the physical and remoteness demands are significant.

title: Biologist, Environmental Compliance Mitigation — Great Plains Region
org: Ducks Unlimited · Fort Collins, CO or Grand Island, NE
deadline: Open until filled (posted July 17, 2026)
body: Develops and delivers wetland, stream, species and habitat mitigation projects for Ducks Unlimited's Environmental Compliance Mitigation program across a seven-state Great Plains region covering ND, SD, NE, KS, CO, WY and MT. Duties include conducting field investigations and wetland delineations, writing mitigation and restoration plans, performing GIS analysis and map production, and coordinating with the Army Corps of Engineers, USFWS, landowners and consultants. The work supports compliance with the Endangered Species Act, NEPA and Clean Water Act Section 404. The posting requires a BS in environmental science or a related field and names the Corps of Engineers Wetland Delineation Manual and Regional Supplements plus GIS analysis among its preferred skills. Salary is posted at $65,000 to $90,000 per year, and the candidate may choose either regional office. The listing describes itself as mid-level with a stated range of two to ten years of experience.
desc: This posting reads as though it were written from Erin's resume — it names the Army Corps Wetland Delineation Manual specifically, pairs it with GIS analysis, and sets the degree bar at a BS in environmental science. Nothing else surfaced this year matches her two strongest technical skills that precisely, and the salary is roughly double what the seasonal technician roles pay. The honest caveat is seniority: Ducks Unlimited calls this mid-level with a two-to-ten-year band, so with a May 2025 BS plus NCCC she sits at or just below the floor and would be the junior candidate in the pool. That makes it a reach rather than a likely offer — but it is exactly the job her next two years should be aimed at, so it is worth applying and worth studying even if it does not land.

Observer roles build rigorous, protocol-driven sampling discipline that agencies value. Two caveats worth carrying: the salary was not published on the listings reached, and because these came via a third-party board rather than a WDFW URL, she should verify which are still open before investing time.

body: Two entry-grade fisheries technician positions on the Cowlitz River. The crew member role supports fish collection, handling, marking and data recording for salmon and steelhead monitoring. The spawning ground surveyor role involves walking and surveying spawning grounds, counting redds and carcasses, and recording biological data for salmon escapement estimates, with extensive stream wading in variable weather. The spawning ground survey role in particular is systematic stream survey work that builds directly on the river fieldwork Erin did for her Pigeon River capstone, just with a different measurement target. Redd and carcass surveys are also a standard technique that appears in countless job descriptions, so learning it widens her options considerably. Both postings share a pay band and deadlines a day apart, so applying to both costs almost nothing extra.

SEFS restoration ecology and wildlife labs use her GIS and camera-trap toolkit directly, and freshwater and watershed systems is a named research area. The Land Stewardship Coordinator writes and updates property management plans across Audubon refuge holdings, conducting field assessment, mapping and report production. The Land Conservation Coordinator focuses on invasive species mapping and management, including field survey, treatment planning and volunteer coordination. The Community Engagement Coordinator runs stormwater outreach programming combining community education with field monitoring and reporting. All three pay a $27,750 living allowance for the term plus a $7,395 Segal Education Award, with health insurance and loan forbearance with interest paid.
desc: All three of these are explicitly entry-level and all three pay $27,750 plus a $7,395 award, which is at the top of the service-term range. The Land Stewardship placement is the strongest fit — writing property management plans from field assessment and mapping is essentially applied GIS plus technical writing, and producing a set of real management plans gives her a portfolio to show. The Land Conservation invasive-mapping role is a close second on the same skills. The stormwater placement pairs her water quality background with outreach. TerraCorps also notes that AmeriCorps alumni are eligible for a second term, so her NCCC service does not disqualify her.

Pay is modest and the term is seasonal.

body: Several openings at a leading California conservation science nonprofit. The Laboratory Research Technician in Petaluma processes and analyzes samples supporting ecology and restoration research at $23.15 per hour with an August 9 deadline. The STRAW Nursery Technician is roughly 75 percent native plant propagation, seed collection, irrigation and nursery inventory plus 20 percent supporting student Restoration Days, at $26.45 per hour with a July 27 deadline and a start after August 31; it asks for two years in habitat restoration, horticulture or natural resource management. Point Blue also runs standing apprenticeship programs: the Palomarin Field Station bird banding and landbird monitoring apprenticeship at Point Reyes, which has trained over 700 early-career scientists since 1966 and posts fall cohorts around September, and the STRAW ecological restoration apprenticeship working on community-based creek and wetland restoration. desc: Point Blue is one of the strongest possible first employers in west coast conservation science, and this cluster gives Erin several angles in. The Laboratory Research Technician role is the cleanest fit — her trace-metal sediment lab work transfers directly, and $23.15 an hour is solid. The STRAW nursery role pays more but asks for two years, which her NCCC service plus field-heavy undergraduate work arguably reaches. The STRAW apprenticeship is the most relevant of the two apprenticeships, since creek and wetland restoration maps onto her delineation training. Palomarin is a famous training program but is bird-banding specific. Apprenticeship pay is unpublished; the contact for Palomarin is Diana Humple.

title: M.S. graduate assistantships — UAF Wildlife Biology, UH Mānoa NREM, and University of Idaho CNR
org: University of Alaska Fairbanks · University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa · University of Idaho
deadline: Contact first — all three route funding through faculty advisors
body: Three advisor-first master's pathways. The University of Alaska Fairbanks Department of Biology and Wildlife supports students through research assistantships including a limited number via the Alaska Cooperative Fish and Wildlife Research Unit and the Institute of Arctic Biology, plus teaching assistantships and fellowships; assistantships may include tuition, stipend, health insurance and fees, paid up to 20 hours per week while school is in session, and students are generally not admitted without a faculty advisor agreeing to mentor them. The University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa NREM program covers sustainable management of tropical terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems including watershed management and ecological restoration; appointments carry a tuition waiver, and the department states funding is limited and often awarded well in advance of application deadlines. University of Idaho's College of Natural Resources offers research assistantships attached to funded projects across fish and wildlife sciences and environmental science, with no central posted list. Stipend amounts are unpublished at all three.
desc: These three share a structure worth understanding: none post a funded MS slot publicly, all three route support through individual faculty, and all three effectively require an advisor to agree before admission. That means the actionable work is faculty emails, not applications. UAF is the strongest technical match — small-mammal trapping and camera-trap monitoring are core methods in its Arctic and boreal wildlife projects, and her NCCC field-conditions experience is a genuine asset there. UH Mānoa's note that funding is often awarded well in advance of deadlines makes a July inquiry unusually well-timed. Idaho's watershed and restoration projects align but its advertised slots skew PhD.

desc: Associate I is the explicit entry grade, and GPS survey data collection is a core duty Erin can already perform rather than something she would learn on the job. Hawaii's invasive species programs are among the most sophisticated in the world out of sheer necessity, so a year there produces genuine specialist knowledge in early detection and rapid response — a framework that transfers to any state. At $3,400 per month the pay is reasonable though Maui's cost of living is high. The terrain is steep and remote and the work is physically demanding, which her NCCC background prepares her for better than most applicants.

body: Two northern New England land trust openings. Blue Hill's posting is notable for explicitly accepting willingness to learn instead of experience, and natural resource inventory work suits Erin's field survey background. Upper Valley's coordinator role pays a real salary of $47,840 to $52,000 and includes genuine easement monitoring and property stewardship duties, which distinguishes it from the organization's other openings that carry a three-year bar. The Upper Valley role is more outreach-weighted.

Available thesis topics include seedbank potential and trophic structure of restored versus unrestored playas. Posted funding is $18,000 per year plus fringe.

The methods list is a good match too: Daubenmire frames and point counts are standard vegetation and wildlife survey techniques close to what she has done, and autonomous recording units extend her camera-trap monitoring experience into acoustics. Comparing restored against unrestored systems is exactly the kind of question that makes a strong thesis and a legible career story. The $18,000 stipend is modest, though Alpine's cost of living is low.

Freshwater mussels are also excellent water quality indicators, which connects to her contamination background. The predictive modeling and ground-truthing component would strengthen her quantitative skills. No prior experience required, and the October 1 deadline leaves real preparation time.

The posting includes a named hiring contact, which is an invitation to call before applying. The technician role is the safer parallel application at the same agency.

The Invasive Species Strike Team Member at the 30,000-acre Trinity River NWR, a bottomland hardwood wetland complex, is a 27-week minimum commitment starting August 2026 at $600 per week with a July 29 deadline. The Invasive Species Support Member at Aransas NWR, wintering ground of the whooping crane, is a 24-week minimum from September 2026 at $600 per week. Trinity River is a bottomland hardwood wetland complex, the closest match to her delineation training. Attwater explicitly wants Field Maps and Survey123, which is her GIS toolkit, plus radio telemetry. The Energy and Minerals placement pays the most at $805 per week with housing help.

GulfCorps, affiliated with The Nature Conservancy, runs crews restoring Gulf Coast habitats out of Houston and Baton Rouge, covering coastal and wetland habitat restoration, invasive species removal and prescribed fire support, with training and certifications provided on a rolling basis. desc: GulfCorps is the stronger of the two for Erin: coastal and wetland habitat restoration under a Nature Conservancy partnership speaks to both the graduate school track and the NGO track, and prescribed fire support adds the burn credential that keeps appearing as a differentiator in agency hiring. At $2,400 to $2,720 per month these are the best-paid corps positions in the Southwest. The education award applying to graduate programs matters given that grad school is her stated direction. Texas Conservation Corps adds disaster response experience, which connects to her post-Hurricane Helene capstone context.
pills: Education award applies to grad school

The DJ&A posting is the standout because it explicitly allows qualifications to be met through education, experience or a combination — that clause is written for someone in Erin's position — and it names a possible transition to full-time. The WestLand role sits inside a consulting division that also does wetlands and GIS work, so it could grow directly into her delineation specialty rather than staying botanical.

title: Bat Research Assistant & Project Manager — multi-year mammal survey program
org: Becker Lab · University of Oklahoma · Norman, OK
deadline: Application deadline August 1, 2026
body: A full-time research assistant and project manager position for long-term studies of migratory and Neotropical bats, managing monthly Mexican free-tailed bat surveys in western Oklahoma, an annual bat survey in northern Belize, and sample exportation from Belize. This is a multi-year appointment starting in early 2027. Posted salary is $36,000 to $39,000 per year.
desc: Mammal field survey and sample handling build directly on Erin's small-mammal trapping training, and bats are simply small mammals with more demanding capture protocols — the transfer is real. What distinguishes this role is the project manager component: running monthly survey programs and coordinating international fieldwork and sample exportation is genuine research management experience that very few people get within two years of a bachelor's degree, and it reads strongly on graduate applications. A multi-year appointment also provides stability. International fieldwork in Belize is a meaningful addition. The salary is modest but the role is salaried and durable.

deadline: Application deadline July 31, 2026

Work spans literature review, environmental data processing, quantitative ecology, and ecological and population modeling. The position suits a student interested in disease ecology and environmental variation. Posted funding is up to $31,200 per year.
desc: At up to $31,200 this is among the best-funded master's positions in the Southwest, and the emphasis is worth noting: environmental data processing, quantitative ecology and population modeling would push Erin toward the analytical side of the field, which is where her GIS skills naturally extend and where the strongest job leverage sits long term. Climate-dependent disease dynamics is also a well-funded research area. The honest caveat is subject: insect vector ecology is a real pivot from wetlands and water quality, so this makes sense if she wants to become a quantitative ecologist and is relatively agnostic about taxon, less so if wetlands are the point.

desc: This is the most remote-sensing-intensive graduate position surfaced in either sweep — LiDAR and drone-based measurement alongside satellite imagery would take Erin's GIS and remote sensing interest from coursework to genuine technical depth, and those are among the most transferable skills in environmental science right now. Entering a PhD directly from a bachelor's with no prior experience required is explicitly allowed here. Forest carbon and species invasion work connects to ecological restoration. The considerations are the same as any direct-to-PhD path: it is a long commitment, and the focus is forestry rather than wetlands, though the methods transfer broadly.
